glossary.definition: A part of computer science that deals with developing hardware and software solutions endowed with human-similar intellectual processes, such as the ability to reason, discover meaning, generalise, or learn from experience.
glossary.sentence: Artificial intelligence deals with developing hardware and software solutions endowed with human-similar intellectual processes, such as the ability to reason, discover meaning, generalise, or learn from experience (Copeland, 2020).

glossary.definition: Underlies systems that think humanly. It considers human being to be an information processing system. Therefore, research works focus on how information is represented, processed, transformed, and stored in the human mind and in computers.
glossary.sentence: A cognitive modelling approach underlies systems that think humanly.

glossary.definition: It is believed that humans, animals and even plants possess intelligence. Human intelligence is studied in-depth in many fields of science, such as philosophy, linguistics, neuroscience, etc. However, it is psychology that has offered a set of theories about the nature of human intelligence and has defined many characteristics of human intelligence. Moreover, this field of science has also developed a set of tests allowing assessment of the level of human intellectual development.
glossary.sentence: To figure out what artificial intelligence is, this concept should be considered in a close connection with our understanding of what is intelligence in general.

glossary.definition: The categories on the right encompass definitions that adopt an ideal intelligence called rationality as a standard.
glossary.sentence: the categories use two standards of intelligent performance (Russell and Norvig, 2009) : the categories on the left include definitions that adopt human intelligence as a standard; the categories on the right encompass definitions that adopt an ideal intelligence called rationality as a standard (Fig.4).

glossary.definition: A test in artificial intelligence intended to assess if a computer system possesses intelligence. It has the following idea. There are 3 rooms. In the first one there is an artificial intelligence, in the second room - a human being or natural intelligence, and in the third room there is a tester (Fig.5). The tester does not see nor artificial intelligence, nor human being and asks questions to both intelligences using a text-based input tool. If the tester cannot differentiate which answers were given by the human being and which ones by the artificial intelligence, than the artificial intelligence is intelligent.
glossary.sentence: The Turing test underlies systems that act humanly and it is also one of the fundamental tests in the field of artificial intelligence intended to assess if a computer system possesses intelligence. It has the following idea. There are 3 rooms. In the first one there is an artificial intelligence, in the second room - a human being or natural intelligence, and in the third room there is a tester (Fig.5). The tester does not see nor artificial intelligence, nor human being and asks questions to both intelligences using a text-based input tool. If the tester cannot differentiate which answers were given by the human being and which ones by the artificial intelligence, than the artificial intelligence is intelligent.

glossary.definition: A sub-field of computer science that involves the development of intelligent computer systems capable of reaching human-level performance in all cognitive tasks.
glossary.sentence: It assumes the development of such intelligent computer systems that can reach human-level performance in all cognitive tasks. Systems acting rationally are based on the approach of rational agents . In this context, an agent is any entity which is capable to perceive its environment and to act within it. A rational agent is one that acts to achieve the best outcome or, when there is uncertainty, the best expected outcome.

glossary.definition: An agent that acts to achieve the best outcome or, when there is uncertainty, the best expected outcome.
glossary.sentence: A rational agent is one that acts to achieve the best outcome or, when there is uncertainty, the best expected outcome.

glossary.definition: A variation of the Turing test that requires the computer to have computer vision to perceive objects, and robotics to manipulate objects and move about.
glossary.sentence: However, the so called the Total Turing test brings forward two more requirements (Russell and Norvig, 2009) : the computer must have computer vision to perceive objects, and robotics to manipulate objects and move about.

glossary.definition: A capability required for a computer system to maintain successful communication with the tester as the human being does in the Turing test.
glossary.sentence: To pass the Turing test, the computer system must have the following capabilities (Russell and Norvig, 2009) : natural language processing for maintaining successful communication with the tester as the human being does; knowledge representation, in order to store information acquired before and during the test.

glossary.definition: the theory and practice of representing knowledge for computer systems. It can be defined more precisely as an incorporation of full range formally described knowledge into an intelligent system with the aim of supporting intelligent performance of the system.
glossary.sentence: In general, “ knowledge representation is the theory and practice of representing knowledge for computer systems” (Jones, 2009). It can be defined more precisely as an incorporation of full range formally described knowledge into an intelligent system with the aim of supporting intelligent performance of the system (Graham and Barrett, 1997).

glossary.definition: A capability required for a computer system to use stored information for answering questions and drawing conclusions.
glossary.sentence: To pass the Turing test, the computer system must have the following capabilities (Russell and Norvig, 2009) : ... automated reasoning, in order to use the stored information for answering questions and drawing conclusions; machine learning, in order to be able to adapt to a new situation, as well as to acquire and generalize patterns of different situations (for example, to a new topic or intent of the tester, etc.).

glossary.definition: A method of data analysis that automates analytical model building. It is a branch of artificial intelligence based on the idea that systems can learn from data, identify patterns and make decisions with minimal human intervention.
glossary.sentence: SAS (n. d.) explains the concept in straightforward words: 'Machine learning is a method of data analysis that automates analytical model building. It is a branch of artificial intelligence based on the idea that systems can learn from data, identify patterns and make decisions with minimal human intervention.'

glossary.definition: A requirement for the Total Turing test that allows the computer to perceive objects.
glossary.sentence: However, the so called the Total Turing test brings forward two more requirements (Russell and Norvig, 2009) : the computer must have computer vision to perceive objects, and robotics to manipulate objects and move about.

glossary.definition: A requirement for the Total Turing test that allows the computer to manipulate objects and move about.
glossary.sentence: However, the so called the Total Turing test brings forward two more requirements (Russell and Norvig, 2009) : the computer must have computer vision to perceive objects, and robotics to manipulate objects and move about.

glossary.definition: A goal related to understanding of those principles which make possible intelligent performance both in natural and artificial systems. To achieve this goal, it is necessary to analyse manifestations of intelligent performance in different types of systems, to formulate and test hypotheses about what it takes to construct intelligent artefacts, as well as designing, building and experimenting with computational systems that are able of demonstrating intelligent performance.
glossary.sentence: Goals of artificial intelligence can be considered along two dimensions (Poole and Mackworth, 2010) : scientific goal is related to understanding of those principles which make possible intelligent performance both in natural and artificial systems. To achieve this goal, it is necessary to analyse manifestations of intelligent performance in different types of systems, to formulate and test hypotheses about what it takes to construct intelligent artefacts, as well as designing, building and experimenting with computational systems that are able of demonstrating intelligent performance; engineering goal is related to the development of useful intelligent artefacts.

glossary.definition: A goal related to the development of useful intelligent artefacts. To achieve these goals, the field of artificial intelligence uses different paradigms, approaches, methods and tasks.
glossary.sentence: Goals of artificial intelligence can be considered along two dimensions (Poole and Mackworth, 2010) : ... engineering goal is related to the development of useful intelligent artefacts. To achieve these goals, the field of artificial intelligence uses different paradigms, approaches, methods and tasks.

glossary.definition: A scientific field that contributed ideas, viewpoints, and techniques to artificial intelligence by considering an idea that the human mind is similar to a physical device that operates knowledge. This knowledge is encoded using some internal language of the device and can be used to find a correct action.
glossary.sentence: Artificial intelligence inherited many of ideas, viewpoints, and techniques from other scientific fields. Its roots lay in the following scientific fields (Russell and Norvig, 2020) : Philosophy - Philosophers made possible artificial intelligence by considering an idea that the human mind is similar to a physical device that operates knowledge. This knowledge is encoded using some internal language of the device and can be used to find a correct action.

glossary.definition: A scientific field that contributed to artificial intelligence through mathematical formalization in three main areas: computing, logics, and probability theory. Mathematicians provided tools to work with logical statements and with uncertain, probabilistic judgments. They also laid the foundations for reasoning algorithms.
glossary.sentence: Artificial intelligence inherited many of ideas, viewpoints, and techniques from other scientific fields. Its roots lay in the following scientific fields (Russell and Norvig, 2020) : ... Mathematics - The transition from the philosophy to the formal science required mathematical formalization in three main areas: computing, logics, and probability theory. Mathematicians provided tools to work with logical statements and with uncertain, probabilistic judgments. They also laid the foundations for reasoning algorithms.

glossary.definition: A scientific field that contributed to artificial intelligence by offering theories on how to make choices that lead to the desired results and laid the grounds for studies of rational agents.
glossary.sentence: Artificial intelligence inherited many of ideas, viewpoints, and techniques from other scientific fields. Its roots lay in the following scientific fields (Russell and Norvig, 2020) : ... Economics - It offered theories on how to make choices that lead to the desired results and laid the grounds for studies of rational agents.

glossary.definition: A scientific field whose contributions in artificial intelligence are related to research on how the human brain processes information. This led to emergence of artificial neural networks which simulate neurons of the human brain and can be used for classification, pattern recognition, optimization, management, and other tasks.
glossary.sentence: Artificial intelligence inherited many of ideas, viewpoints, and techniques from other scientific fields. Its roots lay in the following scientific fields (Russell and Norvig, 2020) : ... Neuroscience – Contributions of neuroscience in artificial intelligence is related to research on how the human brain processes information. This led to emergence of artificial neural networks which simulate neurons of the human brain and can be used for classification, pattern recognition, optimization, management, and other tasks.

glossary.definition: A scientific field that reinforced the idea that humans and animals may be seen as information-processing machines. The main research questions are related to how humans and animals think and act.
glossary.sentence: Artificial intelligence inherited many of ideas, viewpoints, and techniques from other scientific fields. Its roots lay in the following scientific fields (Russell and Norvig, 2020) : ... Psychology - Psychologists reinforced the idea that humans and animals may be seen as information-processing machines. The man research questions are related to how humans and animals think and act.

glossary.definition: A scientific field that provided artefacts which made possible development of artificial intelligence applications. Artificial intelligence applications tend to be complex and large, and they could not work without the progress in computer speed and memory capacity, provided by the computer industry.
glossary.sentence: Artificial intelligence inherited many of ideas, viewpoints, and techniques from other scientific fields. Its roots lay in the following scientific fields (Russell and Norvig, 2020) : ... Computer engineering – Computer engineering provided artefacts, which made possible development of artificial intelligence applications. Artificial intelligence applications tend to be complex and large, and they could not work without the progress in computer speed and memory capacity, provided by the computer industry.

glossary.definition: A scientific field whose main contribution to artificial intelligence is related to the feedback theory, which offered an idea that all intelligent behaviour is the result of feedback mechanism, that is, a mechanism which can be used for the development of useful intelligent artefacts.
glossary.sentence: Artificial intelligence inherited many of ideas, viewpoints, and techniques from other scientific fields. Its roots lay in the following scientific fields (Russell and Norvig, 2020) : ... Control Science and Cybernetics - Feedback theory, which offered an idea that all intelligent behaviour is the result of feedback mechanism, that is, a mechanism which can be used for the development of useful intelligent artefacts.

glossary.definition: A scientific field that contributed ideas, viewpoints, and techniques to artificial intelligence by considering an idea that the human mind is similar to a physical device that operates knowledge. This knowledge is encoded using some internal language of the device and can be used for the development of useful intelligent artefacts.
glossary.sentence: Artificial intelligence inherited many of ideas, viewpoints, and techniques from other scientific fields. Its roots lay in the following scientific fields (Russell and Norvig, 2020) : ... Linguistics - A scientific field that contributed ideas, viewpoints, and techniques to artificial intelligence by considering an idea that the human mind is similar to a physical device that operates knowledge. This knowledge is encoded using some internal language of the device and can be used for the development of useful intelligent artefacts.

Narrow (weak) artificial intelligence , that is also called immature or limited artificial intelligence, is based on the view that intelligence is a set of abilities and is therefore concerned with developing solutions for some specific tasks such as chess playing, medical diagnosis, etc. ( Pennachin and Goertzel , 2007) . glossary.definition: defined by Newell and Simon in 1976. It defines how the general intelligent performance of both humans and computers is achieved and is formulated as follows: “A physical symbol system has the necessary and sufficient means for general intelligent action” (Newell and Simon, 1976) A physical system is capable of carrying out intelligent behaviour only when it is a system of physical symbols (Luger, 2009) .
glossary.sentence: Symbolic artificial intelligence is based on the physical symbol system hypothesis , defined by Newell and Simon in 1976. It defines how the general intelligent performance of both humans and computers is achieved and is formulated as follows: “A physical symbol system has the necessary and sufficient means for general intelligent action” (Newell and Simon, 1976) A physical system is capable of carrying out intelligent behaviour only when it is a system of physical symbols (Luger, 2009) .

glossary.definition: A physical system is capable of carrying out intelligent behaviour only when it is a system of physical symbols (Luger, 2009) . A physical symbol system includes (Bermúdez, 2014; Luger, 2009; Poole and Mackworth, 2010) : symbols that correspond to meaningful physical patterns and could represent any form of knowledge, experience, concepts and causality symbolic structures, which consist of symbols that are connected to each other in some way. Symbolic structures thus depict important aspects of a problem domain; operations (constructing, copying, modifying, and destroying) which allow the use of existing symbolic structures and the creation of new structures; moreover, the operations themselves can be represented by symbols and symbolic structures in the system.
glossary.sentence: A physical system is capable of carrying out intelligent behaviour only when it is a system of physical symbols (Luger, 2009) . A physical symbol system includes (Bermúdez, 2014; Luger, 2009; Poole and Mackworth, 2010) : symbols that correspond to meaningful physical patterns and could represent any form of knowledge, experience, concepts and causality symbolic structures, which consist of symbols that are connected to each other in some way. Symbolic structures thus depict important aspects of a problem domain; operations (constructing, copying, modifying, and destroying) which allow the use of existing symbolic structures and the creation of new structures; moreover, the operations themselves can be represented by symbols and symbolic structures in the system.

glossary.definition: An artificial neural network comprises one or more artificial neurons connected in some pattern (Callan, 2003; Jones, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011).
glossary.sentence: An artificial neural network comprises one or more artificial neurons connected in some pattern (Callan, 2003; Jones, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011). Single neurons are called processing units or nodes. They are connected by transmitting signals to each other.

glossary.definition: John Searle’s Chinese room argument is a famous example of criticism. Searle describes a situation in which the physical system correctly manipulates physical symbols, but the system has no intelligence. The idea of the argument is the following. Imagine a room in which there is a person who receives an input through one slot and produces an output through another slot. Both input and output are represented as a piece of paper with Chinese symbols written on it. The person does not know Chinese and cannot understand what is written. He/she uses an instruction manual written in English that tells the person in the room how to respond to the paper received. Therefore, the only thing the person does is to match the Chinese symbols received with symbols presented in the instruction manual in some sort of formal way – according to their shape, for example. The matching operation allows him/her to identify which symbols to produce as an output. Therefore, the person does not understand the written symbols but is able to provide the right output. To someone outside the room it might look as if there is intelligent behaviour going on. In reality, there is no intelligence.
glossary.sentence: John Searle’s Chinese room argument is a famous example of criticism. Searle describes a situation in which the physical system correctly manipulates physical symbols, but the system has no intelligence. The idea of the argument is the following. Imagine a room in which there is a person who receives an input through one slot and produces an output through another slot. Both input and output are represented as a piece of paper with Chinese symbols written on it. The person does not know Chinese and cannot understand what is written. He/she uses an instruction manual written in English that tells the person in the room how to respond to the paper received. Therefore, the only thing the person does is to match the Chinese symbols received with symbols presented in the instruction manual in some sort of formal way – according to their shape, for example. The matching operation allows him/her to identify which symbols to produce as an output. Therefore, the person does not understand the written symbols but is able to provide the right output. To someone outside the room it might look as if there is intelligent behaviour going on. In reality, there is no intelligence.

glossary.definition: that is also called immature or limited artificial intelligence, is based on the view that intelligence is a set of abilities and is therefore concerned with developing solutions for some specific tasks such as chess playing, medical diagnosis, etc. ( Pennachin and Goertzel , 2007) . This is the only approach in which significant progress has been achieved so far.
glossary.sentence: There are several approaches to artificial intelligence , based on comparing the capabilities of artificial intelligence systems with those of humans. Narrow (weak) artificial intelligence , that is also called immature or limited artificial intelligence, is based on the view that intelligence is a set of abilities and is therefore concerned with developing solutions for some specific tasks such as chess playing, medical diagnosis, etc. ( Pennachin and Goertzel , 2007) . This is the only approach in which significant progress has been achieved so far.

glossary.definition: or, in another words, strong artificial intelligence, which is also called human-level intelligence (Russell and Norvig, 2020) , is an approach treats intelligence as a general capability that can be copied/replicated (Callan, 2003) . It is related to the development of such computer systems that are capable of solving a wide range of tasks in different problem domains and controlling themselves autonomously with their own thoughts, worries, feelings, strengths, weaknesses and predispositions ( Pennachin and Goertzel , 2007) . Thus, this approach attempts to achieve a level of intelligent performance of an intelligent computer system where the system is able to solve a wide range of tasks, including novel tasks, and perform them as well as a human (Russell and Norvig, 2020) . The following necessary characteristics are defined for artificial general intelligence ( Pennachin and Goertzel , 2007) : the ability to solve general problems in a non-domain-restricted way, in the same sense that a human can; the ability to solve problems in particular domains and particular contexts with particular efficiency; the ability to use its more generalized and more specialized intelligence capabilities together, in a unified way; the ability to learn from its environment, other intelligent systems, and teachers (those, who teach such a kind of systems ); the ability to become better at solving novel types of problems as it gains experience with them.
glossary.sentence: Artificial general intelligence or, in another words, strong artificial intelligence, which is also called human-level intelligence (Russell and Norvig, 2020) , is an approach treats intelligence as a general capability that can be copied/replicated (Callan, 2003) . It is related to the development of such computer systems that are capable of solving a wide range of tasks in different problem domains and controlling themselves autonomously with their own thoughts, worries, feelings, strengths, weaknesses and predispositions ( Pennachin and Goertzel , 2007) . Thus, this approach attempts to achieve a level of intelligent performance of an intelligent computer system where the system is able to solve a wide range of tasks, including novel tasks, and perform them as well as a human (Russell and Norvig, 2020) . The following necessary characteristics are defined for artificial general intelligence ( Pennachin and Goertzel , 2007) : the ability to solve general problems in a non-domain-restricted way, in the same sense that a human can; the ability to solve problems in particular domains and particular contexts with particular efficiency; the ability to use its more generalized and more specialized intelligence capabilities together, in a unified way; the ability to learn from its environment, other intelligent systems, and teachers (those, who teach such a kind of systems ); the ability to become better at solving novel types of problems as it gains experience with them.

glossary.definition: is an intelligence that surpasses human capabilities (Russell and Norvig, 2020) and therefore it is much smarter than the best human minds in practically every field, including scientific creativity, general wisdom and social skills (Bostrom, 2015) .
glossary.sentence: Artificial super intelligence is an intelligence that surpasses human capabilities (Russell and Norvig, 2020) and therefore it is much smarter than the best human minds in practically every field, including scientific creativity, general wisdom and social skills (Bostrom, 2015) . Additionally, to the previously mentioned approaches there is also the so called new/nouvelle artificial intelligence . “It focuses on a wide range of organisms, processes, and phenomena that occur at multiple spatial and temporal scales” ( Floreano and Mattiussi, 2008).

glossary.definition: Additionally, to the previously mentioned approaches there is also the so called new/nouvelle artificial intelligence . “It focuses on a wide range of organisms, processes, and phenomena that occur at multiple spatial and temporal scales” ( Floreano and Mattiussi, 2008).
glossary.sentence: Additionally, to the previously mentioned approaches there is also the so called new/nouvelle artificial intelligence . “It focuses on a wide range of organisms, processes, and phenomena that occur at multiple spatial and temporal scales” ( Floreano and Mattiussi, 2008).

glossary.definition: is a paradigm under new artificial intelligence. It considers collective intelligence as a behaviour that emerges through the interaction and cooperation of large numbers of lesser intelligent agents. It includes ant colony optimization , which studies probabilistic algorithms, inspired by ant behaviour when they are searching for food, and particle swarm optimization that studies probabilistic algorithms inspired by birds and fish (Brownlee, 2011) . These two sub-directions are used in search and optimisation.
glossary.sentence: Swarm intelligence is a paradigm under new artificial intelligence. It considers collective intelligence as a behaviour that emerges through the interaction and cooperation of large numbers of lesser intelligent agents. It includes ant colony optimization , which studies probabilistic algorithms, inspired by ant behaviour when they are searching for food, and particle swarm optimization that studies probabilistic algorithms inspired by birds and fish (Brownlee, 2011) . These two sub-directions are used in search and optimisation.

glossary.definition: Artificial intelligence methods that do not use problem-specific knowledge but rely on general methods like search, logic, syntax-based computation, and automatic reasoning.
glossary.sentence: In the first module of the study course, we considered two groups of artificial intelligence methods: weak methods, which do not use knowledge about a problem under consideration, but use general methods like search, logic, syntax-based computation, automatic reasoning, and strong methods, based on the great amount of knowledge about the problem under consideration.

glossary.definition: Artificial intelligence methods that are based on a large amount of knowledge about the problem under consideration, used for incorporating knowledge into an intelligent computer system.
glossary.sentence: In the first module of the study course, we considered two groups of artificial intelligence methods: weak methods, which do not use knowledge about a problem under consideration, but use general methods like search, logic, syntax-based computation, automatic reasoning, and strong methods, based on the great amount of knowledge about the problem under consideration. Incorporating knowledge into an intelligent computer system belongs to the so-called strong methods of artificial intelligence.

glossary.definition: states in a problem-solving process.
glossary.sentence: N is the set of nodes. These correspond to states in a problem-solving process; A is the set of arcs between nodes. These correspond to steps/actions in a problem-solving process; IS is a nonempty subset of N. It contains root nodes or initial state(s) of a problem-solving process; GS is a nonempty subset of N. It contains leaf nodes or goal state(s) of a problem-solving process.

glossary.definition: a process that involves performing actions that lead to transitions between different states. First, we determine the state that we are in at present (the initial state of the problem-solving process). Then, we decide on the state we would like to reach (the goal state of the problem-solving process). To eliminate the discrepancy between these states, we perform actions and pass through a series of intermediate states.
glossary.sentence: You are already aware that a problem-solving process involves performing actions that lead to transitions between different states. First, we determine the state that we are in at present (the initial state of the problem-solving process). Then, we decide on the state we would like to reach (the goal state of the problem-solving process). To eliminate the discrepancy between these states, we perform actions and pass through a series of intermediate states (Figure 2).

glossary.definition: The starting point of the problem-solving process. In tic-tac-toe, it is a blank grid which appears when you start the game.
glossary.sentence: In this game, the initial state of the problem-solving process is a blank grid, which appears when you start the game (Figure 3).

glossary.sentence: Each player wants to reach one of the goal states, in which three marks corresponding to that player are located in a horizontal, vertical or a diagonal row (Figure 4).

glossary.term: zero-sum deterministic two-player games with perfect information
glossary.definition: a relatively simple type of game: zero-sum deterministic two-player games with perfect information . The following aspects characterise this type of game: two players participate in the game and compete against each other. In the context of our course, these could be a human and an intelligent computer system, or two intelligent computer systems playing against each other; the players make alternate moves until the end of the game (i. e. the first player moves, the second player moves, the first player moves, and so on ); both players have access to complete information about the course of the game (including past moves, future moves, and the available objects (e. g. cards or figures) used in the game). Hence, no information is hidden from either player; randomness factors (e. g., the use of a die) are not involved in these types of games; one player’s loss is equal to another player’s gain, giving a total sum of zero.
glossary.sentence: In this course, to demonstrate the application of search-based problem solving to the representation and implementation of game playing, we will focus on a relatively simple type of game: zero-sum deterministic two-player games with perfect information . Hence, no information is hidden from either player; randomness factors (e. g., the use of a die) are not involved in these types of games; one player’s loss is equal to another player’s gain, giving a total sum of zero.

glossary.sentence: Topic 2.2 - A Game Tree - an Application of a State Space Graph In the previous topic, you learnt out that a problem-solving process should be represented in a formal way to ensure a capability of an intelligent computer system to solve problems. A state space graph could be used for this purpose. This topic will explain to you how we can use a state space graph to represent games and thus to create an intelligent computer system that is able to play games against a human or against another intelligent computer system.

glossary.definition: part of the information about the course of the game is hidden from the players (i. e. the players can see only their own cards) and where a die could also be used to introduce randomness.
glossary.sentence: Stochastic multi-player games of imperfect information, such as bridge and poker, in which part of the information about the course of the game is hidden from the players (i. e. the players can see only their own cards) and where a die could also be used to introduce randomness.

glossary.definition: A problem-solving process.
glossary.sentence: Playing a game can be seen as a problem-solving process.

glossary.definition: A game in which the initial state of the problem-solving process is a blank grid, and each player wants to reach one of the goal states, in which three marks corresponding to that player are located in a horizontal, vertical or a diagonal row. To reach a goal state, the players make moves by putting their marks in empty squares.
glossary.sentence: Let's take the game of tic-tac-toe as an example. In this game, the initial state of the problem-solving process is a blank grid, which appears when you start the game (Figure 3). Each player wants to reach one of the goal states, in which three marks corresponding to that player are located in a horizontal, vertical or a diagonal row (Figure 4). To reach a goal state, the players make moves by putting their marks in empty squares.

glossary.definition: Actions taken by players to change the game grid. In tic-tac-toe, each move consists of putting their mark in an empty square.
glossary.sentence: To reach a goal state, the players make moves by putting their marks in empty squares. Each mark added to the game grid changes the appearance of the grid.

glossary.definition: A state that results from making a move. In tic-tac-toe, each possible move produces a specific intermediate state.
glossary.sentence: Each of the possible moves produces a specific intermediate state.

glossary.term: deterministic two-player game of perfect information
glossary.definition: A type of game where moves are made alternately, and node levels are clearly distinguishable in the state space graph. One or more nodes are located at each level, and these display states of the game from which only one of the two players can make a move.
glossary.sentence: Since moves are made alternately in a deterministic two-player game of perfect information, node levels are clearly distinguishable in the state space graph. One or more nodes are located at each level, and these display states of the game from which only one of the two players can make a move.

glossary.definition: A hierarchical layer in the state space graph. In tic-tac-toe, the first level shows the moves from the initial state that can be performed by the player with the X mark; the second level shows the moves of the player with the O mark; and the third level again shows the moves of the player with the X mark.
glossary.sentence: the first level shows the moves from the initial state of the game that can be performed by the player with the X mark; the player with the O mark can perform the moves represented by the nodes in the second level, and the last level in our example again shows the moves of the player with the X mark.

glossary.definition: Characteristics that describe the states in a game. They are related to a computer implementation of a game; they correspond to the properties of the game and its course, in which the values change after making a move during the course of the game; they are not related to the characteristics of the state space graph, such as the number of nodes, the number of levels, the number of arcs, etc.; the values of the parameters allow the players to track the course of the game, since they determine a specific state in the game at a specific moment in time; the values of the parameters should be represented in each node of the state space graph; the parameters depend on the game, and thus may be very different.
glossary.sentence: the parameters that characterise the states in the game . These parameters can be described as follows: they are related to a computer implementation of a game; they correspond to the properties of the game and its course, in which the values change after making a move during the course of the game; they are not related to the characteristics of the state space graph, such as the number of nodes, the number of levels, the number of arcs, etc.; the values of the parameters allow the players to track the course of the game, since they determine a specific state in the game at a specific moment in time; the values of the parameters should be represented in each node of the state space graph; the parameters depend on the game, and thus may be very different;

glossary.definition: A process used by an intelligent computer system to find a solution to a problem. It involves implementing a search algorithm.
glossary.sentence: To allow an intelligent computer system to play the game, we need to implement a search algorithm. Topic 2.3 - State Space Search

glossary.definition: processes the data structure to find a solution to the problem based on known input data (Figure 1). A task of any search algorithm is to find a solution path if such a path exists in the state space graph (Luger, 2009) . To be able to do that, the search algorithm needs to know a search start node and a search goal node, in addition to which, while searching for a solution, it should process a data structure – a state space graph (Figure 2).
glossary.sentence: Additionally, an intelligent computer system needs a search algorithm that processes the data structure to find a solution to the problem based on known input data (Figure 1). A task of any search algorithm is to find a solution path if such a path exists in the state space graph (Luger, 2009) . To be able to do that, the search algorithm needs to know a search start node and a search goal node, in addition to which, while searching for a solution, it should process a data structure – a state space graph (Figure 2).

glossary.definition: the initial node in the state space graph from which the search process begins.
glossary.sentence: The idea of the Hill Climbing algorithm is to always move to the best node in the state space graph. If we imagine that the state space graph with heuristic measures of nodes is similar to a mountain, then the search goal node can be considered to be the top of the mountain, while the search start node is the foot of the mountain (Figure 4). In this case, the Hill Climbing algorithm will always climb up to the best heuristic measure, which in the best case would be defined for the search goal node.

glossary.definition: The algorithm stops when the search goal node is added to the OPEN list or when leaf nodes with heuristic measures better than the heuristic measure of the search goal node are reached.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm stops when the search goal node is added to the OPEN list or when leaf nodes with heuristic measures better than the heuristic measure of the search goal node are reached.

glossary.definition: a path through the state space graph from a given search start node to a given search goal node. It corresponds to an ordered sequence of actions that lead to the solution to the problem. There could be more than one solution path because the same search goal node can be reached by different paths that start at the same search start node.
glossary.sentence: A solution path is a path through the state space graph from a given search start node to a given search goal node. It corresponds to an ordered sequence of actions that lead to the solution to the problem. There could be more than one solution path because the same search goal node can be reached by different paths that start at the same search start node (the fragment is based on information given in (Luger, 2009))

glossary.definition: nodes – the search algorithm has already generated these nodes, but their descendants/ancestors have not yet been generated. Two open nodes - C2 and B1 - appear.
glossary.sentence: Expanding the current node by generating its descendants/ancestors (depending on a search direction). As a result of this action, two types of nodes appear: open nodes – the search algorithm has already generated these nodes, but their descendants/ancestors have not yet been generated; closed nodes – they are nodes whose descendants/ancestors have already been generated. Let’s use our example of the robot in a restricted environment. How are nodes expanded? At the beginning of the search, the current node is the search start node, which is an open node because its descendants have not yet been generated (Figure 6). The search algorithm checks, if the current node is the search goal node. Taking into account that it is not the search goal node, the search algorithm expands it by generating its descendants. As a result of this action, the node C1 becomes a closed node. Two open nodes - C2 and B1 - appear.

glossary.definition: they are nodes whose descendants/ancestors have already been generated. As a result of this action, two types of nodes appear: open nodes – the search algorithm has already generated these nodes, but their descendants/ancestors have not yet been generated; closed nodes – they are nodes whose descendants/ancestors have already been generated.
glossary.sentence: Expanding the current node by generating its descendants/ancestors (depending on a search direction). As a result of this action, two types of nodes appear: open nodes – the search algorithm has already generated these nodes, but their descendants/ancestors have not yet been generated; closed nodes – they are nodes whose descendants/ancestors have already been generated.

glossary.definition: data-driven search (forward chaining ) is one of two types of search in rule-based expert systems.
glossary.sentence: It is possible to perform two types of search in rule-based expert systems (Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 2009; Mallach, 1994; Negnevitsky , 2011; Winston, 1992): data-driven search (forward chaining ); goal-driven search (backward chaining).

glossary.definition: forward chaining is a technique for gathering information and then inferring from it whatever can be inferred.
glossary.sentence: Forward chaining is a technique for gathering information and then inferring from it whatever can be inferred ( Negnevitsky , 2011). It is typically used for problems of interpretation because, in this type of problems, most of the data are initially given, and the system searches for their interpretation (Luger, 2009; Hopgood, 2001).

glossary.definition: goal-driven search (backward chaining) is one of two types of search in rule-based expert systems.
glossary.sentence: It is possible to perform two types of search in rule-based expert systems (Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 2009; Mallach, 1994; Negnevitsky , 2011; Winston, 1992): data-driven search (forward chaining ); goal-driven search (backward chaining).

glossary.definition: the search process starts from a leaf node and it proceeds in backward direction in the state space graph. This is called goal-driven search or backward chaining .
glossary.sentence: From a goal back to the data. This is called goal-driven search or backward chaining . The search process starts from a leaf node and it proceeds in backward direction in the state space graph.

glossary.definition: The OPEN list always contains the same number of nodes as the beam width, excluding: iteration 0, when only the search start node is added to the OPEN list, and a case when the number of direct descendants/ancestors is less than the beam width.
glossary.sentence: Other nodes are placed on the CLOSED list. Therefore, the OPEN list always contains the same number of nodes as the beam width, excluding: iteration 0, when only the search start node is added to the OPEN list, and a case when the number of direct descendants/ancestors is less than the beam width.

glossary.definition: Other nodes are placed on the CLOSED list.
glossary.sentence: Other nodes are placed on the CLOSED list. Therefore, the OPEN list always contains the same number of nodes as the beam width, excluding: iteration 0, when only the search start node is added to the OPEN list, and a case when the number of direct descendants/ancestors is less than the beam width.

glossary.definition: explored the state space graph through a principle of deepening. Let’s consider another way of searching the state space graph. Breadth-First Search explores the state space graph in a level-by-level fashion.
glossary.sentence: Both algorithms considered so far—Depth-First Search and Depth-Limited Search—explored the state space graph through a principle of deepening.

glossary.definition: overcomes a problem of going along indefinitely deep paths. At the same time, it is not able to find a solution path if the search goal node is located below the depth limit.
glossary.sentence: Depth-Limited Search overcomes a problem of going along indefinitely deep paths. At the same time, it is not able to find a solution path if the search goal node is located below the depth limit.

glossary.definition: the depth limit should be provided before the algorithm starts its operation.
glossary.sentence: the depth limit should be provided before the algorithm starts its operation.

glossary.definition: the depth level of the search start node is equal to 0.
glossary.sentence: the depth level of the search start node is equal to 0.

glossary.definition: a machine learning algorithm performs mathematical operations to the given data according to the model specified with the aim of finding the best values of parameters to learn a prediction, solve a classification task or find clusters of data.
glossary.sentence: However, after that, a machine learning algorithm performs mathematical operations to the given data according to the model specified with the aim of finding the best values of parameters to learn a prediction, solve a classification task or find clusters of data.

glossary.definition: explores the state space graph in a level-by-level fashion. This idea is represented in the title of the algorithm (Figure 14): “breadth” means to consider nodes sequentially at the same level before going deeper; “first” indicates finding the first path that leads to the search goal node and stopping the operation.
glossary.sentence: Breadth-First Search explores the state space graph in a level-by-level fashion. This idea is represented in the title of the algorithm (Figure 14): “breadth” means to consider nodes sequentially at the same level before going deeper; “first” indicates finding the first path that leads to the search goal node and stopping the operation.

glossary.definition: The algorithm performs a search simultaneously in both directions - data-driven search and goal-driven search - until it finds a point of contact in both directions.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm reduces the consumption of computational resources (search time and computer memory), which is the main problem with Breadth-First Search. The algorithm performs a search simultaneously in both directions - data-driven search and goal-driven search - until it finds a point of contact in both directions. The difference between Breadth-First Search and Bidirectional Search is evident if we use the analogy of the state space graph and tree crown (Figure 21). Breadth-First Search explores closer branches and then goes to the ends of branches. Bidirectional Search starts simultaneously from the search start node and the search goal node. It explores closer branches of both nodes until it finds a point of contact. The algorithm uses two OPEN lists and two CLOSED lists. The lists are filled in simultaneously in both directions. Bidirectional Search includes an additional operational step (Figure 22). In each iteration, after expanding the current nodes, it checks if there is at least one common node in both OPEN lists. The algorithm stops if a common node is found or if the state space is fully searched, but a common node is not found. Let us consider an example of Bidirectional Search by implementing search on the state space graph that you see in Figure 23, taking into account that the search start node is A and the search goal node is K. We will use two OPEN lists and two CLOSED lists in this algorithm. Iteration 0: The search start node - the node A - is added to the OPEN list of data-driven search. The search goal node - the node K - is added to the OPEN list of goal-driven search. Both CLOSED lists are empty because none of the nodes has been expanded yet. The algorithm checks if there is at least one common node in both OPEN lists. The common node is not found. Nodes located on the first positions of the OPEN lists become the current nodes. The algorithm checks if the current node A is the search goal node in the data-driven search and if the current node K is the search start node in the goal-driven search. This checking gives negative result. The algorithm starts the next iteration. Iteration 1: The algorithm expands the node A and the node K simultaneously. The descendants of the node A are the nodes D, H, and E. None of them have been added to the OPEN list or to the CLOSED list of data-driven search. Thus, all three nodes are added to the OPEN list in the sequence that they were found. The node A is moved to the CLOSED list as an already expanded node. The ancestors of the node K are the nodes I, E, F, and G. None of them have been added to the OPEN list or to the CLOSED list of goal-driven search. Thus, all four nodes are added to the OPEN list in the sequence that they were found. The node K is moved to the CLOSED list as an already expanded node. The algorithm checks if there is at least one common node in both OPEN lists. The node E is in both lists; thus, the algorithm stops its operation. Bidirectional Search consumes less computational resources than Breadth-First Search. Moreover, it is also able to find the shortest path to the search goal node. Unfortunately, Bidirectional Search has limited usage because it needs to know exactly the search goal node, which is impossible in many practical applications. Moreover, sometimes this algorithm is not able to find a point of contact of the two search directions due to very different average numbers of connections per node in both directions.

glossary.definition: It is used for solving complex problems with incomplete information when it is not possible to describe or evaluate the problem in full detail. However, humans also have a lot of heuristic knowledge related to routine (daily) situations.
glossary.sentence: It is used for solving complex problems with incomplete information when it is not possible to describe or evaluate the problem in full detail. However, humans also have a lot of heuristic knowledge related to routine (daily) situations It is used for solving a) problems that may not have an exact solution because of inherent ambiguities in the problem statement or available data or b) problems that may have an exact solution, but there are not enough computational resources to solve them What drawbacks does it have? The application of human heuristic knowledge could be fallible and may not give a solution, because every individual has different experiences, and rules based on heuristic knowledge are inherently individual The application of heuristic evaluation function can lead to the best solution but can also lead to only an acceptable solution or to no solution at all Heuristic knowledge is applied in many areas of computer science, including game development, theorem proving, medical diagnosis systems, computer vision systems, financial systems, bioinformatics, and antivirus software. Taking into account that heuristic knowledge is represented in intelligent computer systems as a mathematical function, let’s consider this function. Heuristic knowledge in intelligent computer systems is represented as a mathematical function called a heuristic evaluation function . This function helps the search algorithm to determine the direction of the location of the search goal node (Poole, 2010). The purpose of the function is to find a problem solution with minimum costs (Ertel, 2011). Let’s consider the idea of a heuristic evaluation function by contrasting two worlds: the real world and the world of intelligent computer systems (Figure 1). In the real world, we, as humans, have a lot of problems to solve in our everyday or professional lives, such as playing games, managing our finances, and addressing medical issues. To solve problems, we use problem-specific knowledge called heuristic knowledge that very often is based on our experience. This knowledge depends on the nature of the problem. In the case of playing games, we have a specific game strategy that allows us to choose the most promising moves to make. In financial management, heuristic knowledge includes knowing favourable conditions for investments or buying currency. Doctors have heuristic knowledge about relationships between different symptoms and diagnoses. When we are building an intelligent computer system, we represent a problem-solving process as a state space graph. Heuristic knowledge is incorporated in the heuristic evaluation function, which calculates numerical values called heuristic measures . Heuristic measures are assigned to nodes of the state space graph. This means that the heuristic knowledge that we usually formulate verbally should be represented mathematically and made accessible by the intelligent computer system through heuristic measures. Heuristic measures represent the quality of the nodes. How to measure the quality of the nodes in a state space graph is an important question. In other words, how do we relate the heuristic knowledge that we have in our life to the nodes of a state space graph? In the state space graph, we have only nodes and the arcs connecting them. The search start node and the search goal node are known for the search purpose. Imagine that in the search process, the algorithm has generated three descendants of the current node C (Figure 2). How can we evaluate that one node, for example, E, is better or more qualitative than others, for example, D and F? The analysis of the nodes shows the following: the node D does not lead to the search goal node at all; the node F leads to the search goal node, but paths that include this node are longer in the terms of the number of arcs than a path through the node E. The quality of nodes is measured by two factors: the possibility of reaching the search goal node from the given node n or, in other words, checking if the given node n is located on graph paths leading to the search goal node, and the possibility of reaching the search goal node from the given node n by going along the best (usually the shortest) path. This idea is represented in an abstract way as follows (Luger, 2009): f(n) = g(n) + h(n), where n – a node under consideration; g(n) – the distance between the search start node and node n; this value allows the algorithm to choose the shorter paths that are available; h(n) – a heuristic measure that characterizes the quality of node n or, in other words, the estimation of the distance between the node n and the search goal node; this value allows the algorithm to choose promising paths in a state space graph. However, in practical applications, the previously mentioned elements of the heuristic evaluation function are represented indirectly by focusing on factors characterizing states in the problem-solving process and their importance for reaching the goal state (Figure 3). As a result, the heuristic evaluation function can be represented as a polynomial function, as used by Samuel in his checker program ((Samuel, 1959) as cited in (Luger, 2009)): where n – a node under consideration; ai – the weight of the ith factor; xi – the heuristic measure of the ith factor characterizing states in the problem-solving process. Let’s consider examples of two games demonstrating the idea of problem-specific factors and their numerical representation. Imagine we are building an intelligent computer system that will play a game of Tic-tac-toe. The system has a state space graph of the game. In this demonstration we will consider only the initial state of the game and its three descendants (Figure 4). To evaluate nodes in the graph, the system can apply a heuristic evaluation function based on such a factor as the number of potentially winning positions for the computer. The computer starts the game. It needs to select the most promising move. The system generates descendants of the initial state of the game and applies the defined heuristic evaluation function. Any state where the computer can place its sign in a corner receives a value of 3 as there are 3 potentially winning positions. The state where the computer can place its sign in the centre receives a value of 4 as there are 4 potentially winning positions. The last possible move receives a value of 2. Therefore, the intelligent computer system will select to place its sign into the centre as such a move maximizes its chances to win. Such an approach is used at any level when the computer needs to make a move. (The paragraph is based on the description given in (Luger, 2009)) Another example is the game of 8-puzzle. There are 8 numbered tiles and 9 squares. Players are allowed to move a tile onto an empty square with the aim of achieving the goal state that you can see in Figure 5. We will consider only three states generated from the initial state of the game. The simplest factor to consider is the number of the tiles out of place in each state when it is compared with the goal state (Figure 6). The logic behind such a heuristic evaluation function is quite simple: the state that has the fewest tiles out of place is probably closer to the goal state. Applying the defined factor, the first and the third node will acquire a value of 5, while the second node will get a value of 3, and therefore, this could be considered a promising move. (The paragraph is based on the description given in (Luger, 2009)) Another factor that can be used to evaluate nodes is to sum up distances of tiles from their intended places (Figure 7).

glossary.definition: A function used to find the most promising moves, which is applied during the exploration of the game states.
glossary.sentence: They can be based on the principles of either the Minimax or Alpha-beta pruning, and use a heuristic evaluation function to find the most promising moves. ... It applies a heuristic evaluation function and acquires heuristic measures for the terminal nodes. ... Both strategies described above use a heuristic evaluation function.

glossary.definition: the average number of arcs per node in the state space graph, calculated by dividing the sum of direct descendants of all nodes with the total number of nodes
glossary.sentence: B – the branching factor of the state space graph . It is the average number of arcs per node. Usually, we acquire this factor by dividing the sum of direct descendants of all nodes with the total number of nodes in the state space graph.

glossary.definition: the length of the longest path in the state space graph, usually measured as the number of arcs
glossary.sentence: L – depth of the state space graph (usually the length of the longest path).

glossary.definition: A problem that occurs when a heuristic evaluation function with limited look-ahead may not detect a heuristically promising path, leading to a bad situation later in the game.
glossary.sentence: One of the most significant problems with this approach is called the horizon effect (Jones, 2009; Luger, 2009). When a heuristic evaluation function is applied with limited look-ahead, it is possible that the depth of the look-ahead may not detect a heuristically promising path, and this may lead to a bad situation later in the game.

glossary.definition: the number of pairs of tiles located close to each other that need to be swapped in order to reach the search goal node, usually requiring more than two moves
glossary.sentence: We can define one more factor for this game: the number of pairs of tile reversals (Figure 8). This means that we need to change the places of two tiles located close one to another, and usually, it takes more than two moves to put them back.

glossary.definition: a factor used in heuristic evaluation functions where the smaller the sum of distances, the closer a state is to the search goal node
glossary.sentence: The logic is also very similar here: the smaller the sum of distances, the closer a state is to the search goal node.

glossary.term: number of tiles out of place
glossary.definition: a factor used in heuristic evaluation functions that counts how many tiles are not in their correct positions relative to the search goal node
glossary.sentence: We can use each of them alone as a heuristic evaluation function, for example, f(n) = the number of tiles out of place f(n) = the sum of distances f(n) = the number of tile reversals.

glossary.definition: For univariate linear regression, there is a unique solution because the weights of the model can be calculated using the following equations (Russell and Norvig, 2010): However, calculating the weights for the case of multiple input features is not a trivial task. There is also a closed-form solution like this one: However, this solution is not used in practice because the computation of matrices can fail if there is a problem of matrix singularity. Moreover, the computational complexity of inverting a multiplication of a transpose of vector X and vector X is very high (Geron, 2017). Additionally, calculating the loss function for every possible value of each weight over the entire dataset would be an inefficient way of finding the convergence point (Google Developers, n.d.). Therefore, usually, another mechanism is used, which is called gradient descent. The gradient descent is an optimisation technique used to measure changes in the output regarding changes in input values. The gradient descent is based on calculating a partial derivative with respect to all independent variables. In the case of Linear regression, the changing output is a value of the cost function across the whole dataset, input values are weight values of the linear function and independent variables are presented by a feature vector. The gradient descent technique thus allows the cost function value to be minimised according to changes in the values of the weights of the linear function. Actually, you do not need to know the calculus underlying the gradient descent in detail because the tools, frameworks and languages that exist for using linear regression will automatically handle these computations. However, if you are interested in more information on gradient descent, please, use these sources: To apply the gradient descent, a hyperparameter α called learning rate is used. It represents a rate at which Linear regression updates the weights of the linear function.

glossary.sentence: The factors included in the function and their weights will be determined by the system’s developer in collaboration with domain experts and will be validated using machine learning and optimization techniques.

glossary.definition: systems that use heuristic evaluation functions to solve problems when computational resources are limited
glossary.sentence: Heuristic knowledge is often used in the development of intelligent computer systems for problems that may have an exact solution, but there are not enough computational resources to find that solution.

glossary.definition: the complexity of a state space graph depends on two factors: branching factor and depth
glossary.sentence: In general, the complexity of a state space graph depends on two factors (Luger, 2009): B – the branching factor of the state space graph . It is the average number of arcs per node.

glossary.definition: the hardware and software capabilities available to process and store information in intelligent computer systems
glossary.sentence: It is not possible to store and process the whole graph due to limited computational resources.

glossary.definition: A value assigned to a node based on its evaluation, which represents an estimate of the node's utility in the context of the game being played.
glossary.sentence: It assigns a precise heuristic measure of “1” to the leaf node.

glossary.definition: an algorithm that always moves to the best node in the state space graph. If we imagine that the state space graph with heuristic measures of nodes is similar to a mountain, then the search goal node can be considered to be the top of the mountain, while the search start node is the foot of the mountain (Figure 4). In this case, the Hill Climbing algorithm will always climb up to the best heuristic measure, which in the best case would be defined for the search goal node.
glossary.sentence: The idea of the Hill Climbing algorithm is to always move to the best node in the state space graph. If we imagine that the state space graph with heuristic measures of nodes is similar to a mountain, then the search goal node can be considered to be the top of the mountain, while the search start node is the foot of the mountain (Figure 4). In this case, the Hill Climbing algorithm will always climb up to the best heuristic measure, which in the best case would be defined for the search goal node.

glossary.definition: search algorithms that receive the search start node and the search goal node as input data, the state space graph, a data structure, is used to search for a problem solution, and they provide a solution path as their output. They have three main operational steps: selecting the current node; checking if the current node is the search goal node; expanding the current node if it is not the search goal node.
glossary.sentence: In Module 2 of our course, we considered the operational steps of uninformed search algorithms. They receive the search start node and the search goal node as input data, the state space graph, a data structure, is used to search for a problem solution, and they provide a solution path as their output. They have three main operational steps (Figure 1): selecting the current node; checking if the current node is the search goal node; expanding the current node if it is not the search goal node.

glossary.definition: search algorithms that receive an additional input - the principles of calculation of the heuristic measures of nodes or, in other words, a heuristic evaluation function. glossary.definition: is reward-based learning. According to (Atul, 2019), it can be characterised as "My life-my rules!". Reinforcement learning is suitable for agents (like robots, self-driving cars, or a game- playing software) who operate in a changing environment and, to act in the environment, they need to find the best sequence of actions that maximise the end goal (Pant, 2019). Therefore, the agent learns how to act in the environment. The agent receives feedback: a reward if it acts correctly and a penalty if it acts incorrectly (Geron, 2017; Pant, 2019). The reward is represented as a numerical value but it is given with some delay, like at the end of the game when it is known who has won the game: computer or human. The agent learns without intervention from human, for example, the two points for a win at the end of a chess game tells the agent it did something right and, thus, it is up to the agent to decide which of the actions before the reinforcement were most responsible for it (Russell & Norvig, 2010). For example, DeepMind's AlphaGo program learned its optimal gaming policy by analysing millions of games and then playing many games against itself (Geron, 2017).

glossary.definition: Therefore, usually, another mechanism is used, which is called gradient descent. The gradient descent is an optimisation technique used to measure changes in the output regarding changes in input values. The gradient descent is based on calculating a partial derivative with respect to all independent variables. In the case of Linear regression, the changing output is a value of the cost function across the whole dataset, input values are weight values of the linear function and independent variables are presented by a feature vector. The gradient descent technique thus allows the cost function value to be minimised according to changes in the values of the weights of the linear function. Actually, you do not need to know the calculus underlying the gradient descent in detail because the tools, frameworks and languages that exist for using linear regression will automatically handle these computations. glossary.definition: Suppose values of all features in the dataset are continuous. glossary.definition: a method to calculate the distance between a new data object and existing ones in the dataset, used in the kNN algorithm.
glossary.sentence: calculate the distance (based on Euclidean, Manhattan or Hamming distance) between a new data object whose output value should be determined and the current data object from the dataset;

glossary.definition: a method to calculate the distance between two categorical feature values in the kNN algorithm, counting the number of features where they differ.
glossary.sentence: for categorical feature values, usually, the Hamming distance is used that counts the number of features in which two data objects differ: the fewer the differences in features, the greater the similarity of data objects;

glossary.definition: a parameter in the kNN algorithm that defines the number of neighbours considered for making predictions.
glossary.sentence: it is necessary to emphasise that before you start using the algorithm, you need to define the value of the hyperparameter k , which represents the chosen number of neighbours that will be examined.

glossary.definition: K-nearest neighbour or kNN algorithm is a very simple supervised learning algorithm that can solve both classification and regression tasks.
glossary.sentence: K-nearest neighbour or kNN algorithm is a very simple supervised learning algorithm that can solve both classification and regression tasks.

glossary.definition: In the kNN version of the algorithm, a hyperparameter k is used. It defines the number of nearest data objects that will be considered to identify the output value for a new data object. The developer sets a value of the hyperparameter before the operation of the algorithm. The algorithm applies a voting mechanism ; that is, after finding distances of data objects in the dataset to a new data object and sorting data objects from the dataset in ascending order of the calculated distances, the algorithm selects k data objects with smaller distances and take the plurality vote of the neighbours (which is the majority vote in the case of binary classification) (Russell & Norvig, 2010). A new data object receives a label of the class with the majority vote.
glossary.sentence: In the kNN version of the algorithm, a hyperparameter k is used. The developer sets a value of the hyperparameter before the operation of the algorithm. A new data object receives a label of the class with the majority vote.

glossary.definition: In multiclass classification, using an odd number of nearest neighbours does not prevent ties. The developer needs to define a mechanism to choose between different classes if the number of data objects belonging to each of them is equal (Kubat, 2017).

glossary.definition: a measure used in clustering to determine how similar two data objects are, depending on their feature types (categorical or continuous).
glossary.sentence: the concept of similarity was considered in detail in the second topic of the module in regard to the kNN algorithm. It is also used in unsupervised machine learning.

glossary.definition: the rate at which the algorithm makes incorrect predictions, used to determine the optimal k value in the kNN algorithm.
glossary.sentence: It is practical to create a plot between error rate and k values, later choosing the k value providing a minimum error rate.

glossary.definition: the process of scaling feature values to ensure they contribute equally to the distance calculations in the kNN algorithm.

glossary.definition: a type of machine learning task where the algorithm predicts continuous numerical values for new data objects, using the mean of the k labels in the kNN algorithm.
glossary.sentence: in the regression task, it returns the mean of the k labels.

glossary.definition: the average of the current set of feature vectors within a cluster, around which data objects are grouped based on their distance to the centroid in the K-Means algorithm.
glossary.sentence: A centroid is a centre of a cluster around which data objects are grouped based on their distance to the centroid. It is the average of the current set of feature vectors within the cluster (Jones, 2009).

glossary.definition: an unsupervised machine learning algorithm that groups data into K clusters by minimising the sum of distances between data objects and their respective centroids.
glossary.sentence: The K-Means algorithm is one of the popular algorithms of unsupervised machine learning.

glossary.definition: a subset of data objects within a dataset that are grouped based on their similarity to each other and differences from other clusters.
glossary.sentence: These subsets of data are called clusters ; for example, an enterprise can group its clients based on the level of income (clients with high, average, and low income),

glossary.definition: representations of data objects that contain all relevant features, used in clustering algorithms like K-Means and hierarchical clustering.
glossary.sentence: an available dataset (data objects represented by feature vectors) into subsets that share some similarities.

glossary.definition: the number of clusters specified as a hyperparameter in the K-Means algorithm before the clustering process begins.
glossary.sentence: Specify the number of clusters, K , that need to be generated by this algorithm. Thus, K is the hyperparameter of this algorithm.

glossary.definition: the average feature vector values of a cluster calculated during the K-Means algorithm, which are used to assign data objects to clusters and update centroids until convergence.
