The typical termination criteria of the clustering process in the K-Means algorithm is an iteration when data objects do not change their cluster membership (centroid values do not change) (Jones, 2009; Kubat, 2017; Tyugu , 2007) .

glossary.definition: an unsupervised machine learning algorithm that builds a hierarchy of clusters by recursively merging or dividing them, without requiring the number of clusters to be specified beforehand.
glossary.sentence: Hierarchical clustering is another unsupervised machine learning algorithm that does not demand from the developer any assumption on the number of clusters as the K-Means algorithm does.

glossary.definition: Each data object initially is attributed to its own cluster. Then, at each level, a selected pair of clusters is recursively merged into a single cluster. This produces a grouping at the next higher level with one less cluster. The pairs chosen for merging consist of the two groups with the smallest intergroup dissimilarity. Therefore, a pair of clusters is merged at each hierarchy level until one cluster with all data objects is acquired at the highest level.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, each data object initially is attributed to its own cluster. Then, at each level, a selected pair of clusters is recursively merged into a single cluster. This produces a grouping at the next higher level with one less cluster. The pairs chosen for merging consist of the two groups with the smallest intergroup dissimilarity (the fragment is based on the information given in (Hastie, 2017)) . Therefore, a pair of clusters is merged at each hierarchy level until one cluster with all data objects is acquired at the highest level. Figure 2 represents the process of agglomerative hierarchical clustering in which clusters having the smallest distance between them are merged at each step of clustering.

glossary.definition: Clades that represent merging of data objects into clusters; each clade has its height; leaves that are terminal of clades corresponding to the data objects used in the clustering process.
glossary.sentence: A dendrogram consists of (Figure 5): clades that represent merging of data objects into clusters; each clade has its height; leaves that are terminal of clades corresponding to the data objects used in the clustering process. The key to interpreting a dendrogram is to focus on the height of clades, that is, the height at which two data objects are merged.

glossary.definition: In deciding about the cluster similarity, the distance between the clusters' most distant elements (the longest distance) is calculated: where G, H – clusters; d – distance; i – a data object from the cluster G; i' – a data object from the cluster H.
glossary.sentence: The most used methods are (Hastie, 2017) : Complete-linkage : in deciding about the cluster similarity, the distance between the clusters’ most distant elements (the longest distance) is calculated: where G, H – clusters; d – distance; i – a data object from the cluster G; i' – a data object from the cluster H.

glossary.definition: A horizontal cut- off line is usually made through the dendrogram to decide the number of clusters based on the hierarchical clustering algorithm. The number of vertical lines intersected by the horizontal cut- off line represents the number of clusters.
glossary.sentence: A horizontal cut- off line is usually made through the dendrogram to decide the number of clusters based on the hierarchical clustering algorithm. The number of vertical lines intersected by the horizontal cut- off line represents the number of clusters.

glossary.definition: It is a tree-like diagram representing hierarchical relationships between data objects in the dataset. According to (Hastie, 2017) , “a dendrogram provides a highly interpretable complete description of the hierarchical clustering in a graphical format” . Thus, it provides insight into the way how clusters were formed.
glossary.sentence: A dendrogram is an output of hierarchical clustering. It is a tree-like diagram representing hierarchical relationships between data objects in the dataset. According to (Hastie, 2017) , “a dendrogram provides a highly interpretable complete description of the hierarchical clustering in a graphical format” . Thus, it provides insight into the way how clusters were formed.

glossary.definition: Divisive hierarchical clustering applies a top-down clustering approach. Therefore, it starts with a cluster with all data objects inside it. Then, at each hierarchy level, it recursively splits one of the existing clusters into two new clusters. The split is chosen to produce two new groups with the largest between-group dissimilarity (the fragment is based on the information given in (Hastie, 2017)) . Finally, the clustering stops when each data object makes a cluster.
glossary.sentence: Divisive hierarchical clustering applies a top-down clustering approach. Therefore, it starts with a cluster with all data objects inside it. glossary.definition: There are several methods to measure the similarity (distance) between clusters to decide the rules for merging clusters, and they are often called linkage methods . The most used methods are (Hastie, 2017) : Complete-linkage : in deciding about the cluster similarity, the distance between the clusters’ most distant elements (the longest distance) is calculated: where G, H – clusters; d – distance; i – a data object from the cluster G; i' – a data object from the cluster H. Single-linkage : in deciding about the cluster similarity, the distance between the closest elements of the two clusters (the shortest distance) is calculated: where G, H – clusters; d – distance; i – a data object from the cluster G; i' – a data object from the cluster H. It represents the harmonic mean of precision and recall because the classifier will only get a high F1 score if both recall and precision are high (Geron, 2017) . Thus, it estimates the number of data objects classified correctly.

glossary.definition: A precision metric evaluates a fraction of predicted positives that are true positives. In other words, the accuracy of the positive predictions (Geron, 2017) . Precision is the probability that the classifier is right when labelling an example as positive (Kubat, 2017) . It is calculated in the following way (Kubat, 2017; Geron, 2017) : The value of precision lies in the range [0,1]. A larger value indicates better predictive accuracy.
glossary.sentence: A precision metric evaluates a fraction of predicted positives that are true positives

glossary.definition: A recall metric (also called sensitivity and true positive rate) evaluates a fraction of data objects classified as positives out of the total actual positive data objects. According to (Kubat, 2017) , recall is the probability that a positive data object will be correctly recognised by the algorithm. It is calculated in the following way (Kubat, 2017; Geron, 2017) : The value of recall lies in the range [0,1]. A larger value indicates better predictive accuracy.
glossary.sentence: A recall metric (also called sensitivity and true positive rate)

glossary.definition: The behaviour of the classifier under different values of its parameters can be visualised by the so-called ROC (The receiver operating characteristic) curve (Kubat, 2017) . The ROC curve plots false positive rate (x-axis) versus true positive rate (y-axis) for each value of the hyperparameter, helping you visualise values that would be good choices for the tradeoff (Russell & Norvig, 2010; Geron, 2017; Kubat, 2017) . The ideal case is represented by a situation when the curve reaches the upper-left corner that represents zero error rate on the false positives and 100% accuracy on the true positives (Kubat, 2017) . It can be concluded that the worst-case scenario (random chance) consists of a 45 degrees diagonal line. ROC curve can be calculated for every class in multiclass classification versus all other classes.
glossary.sentence: The behaviour of the classifier under different values of its parameters can be visualised by the so-called ROC (The receiver operating characteristic) curve

glossary.definition: One of the metrics that do not demand ground-truth labels is the Silhouette Score . The Silhouette Score is calculated by the following way: where a - average intra-cluster distance (the average distance between each point within a cluster), b - average inter-cluster distance (the average distance between all clusters). Therefore, it represents the closeness of each data object in a cluster to data objects in the neighbouring clusters.
glossary.sentence: One of the metrics that do not demand ground-truth labels is the Silhouette Score

glossary.definition: In the multiclass classification task, we first calculate a precision for each class, taking the value on the diagonal as true positives and the sum of other values in the column corresponding to the class as false positives.
glossary.sentence: taking the value on the diagonal as true positives

glossary.definition: Area under the ROC Curve (AUC) allows comparing different classifiers. It provides a single-number summary of the ROC curve (Russell & Norvig, 2010) . It measures the entire two-dimensional area underneath the entire ROC curve. It returns a value in the range [0, 1]. A highly accurate classifier will have a ROC AUC equal to 1; in contrast, a purely random classifier will have a ROC AUC equal to 0.5 (Geron, 2017) . AUC can be calculated for each class vs other classes for the multiclass classification task and then averaged across all classes.
glossary.sentence: Area under the ROC Curve (AUC) allows comparing different classifiers

glossary.definition: A feed-forward network has connections only in one dir • ection . Every processing unit receives input from "upstream" processing units and delivers output to "downstream" processing units; there are no loops. Thus, a feed-forward network represents a function of its current input.

glossary.definition: Learning in artificial neural networks is related to modifying weights in some particular way . It means that after processing the input signals in the whole network, the artificial neural network receives a final output which in the case of supervised machine learning can be compared with the desired (actual label) output. In case if there is a difference between the desired output and the output of the network, a learning rule is applied to modify the weights of the network.

glossary.definition: Perceptron Rosenblatt developed the perceptron model in the late 1950s (Jones, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011). A Single Layer Perceptron is the simplest and the commonly known type of artificial neural network, but its practical applicability is limited because it can learn only linearly separable functions.

A Single Layer Perceptron is the simplest and the commonly known type of artificial neural network, but its practical applicability is limited because it can learn only linearly separable functions.

glossary.definition: A recurrent network , on the other hand, feeds its outputs back into its inputs. It means that the activation levels of the network form a dynamic system that may reach a stable state or exhibit oscillations or even chaotic behaviour. Moreover, the network's response to a given input depends on its initial state, which may depend on previous inputs.
glossary.sentence: Based on the way of connecting neurons, two types of artificial neural networks can be distinguished (Russell & Norvig, 2010): A feed-forward network has connections only in one dir • ection . Every processing unit receives input from "upstream" processing units and delivers output to "downstream" processing units; there are no loops. Thus, a feed-forward network represents a function of its current input. A recurrent network , on the other hand, feeds its outputs back into its inputs. It means that the activation levels of the network form a dynamic system that may reach a stable state or exhibit oscillations or even chaotic behaviour. Moreover, the network's response to a given input depends on its initial state, which may depend on previous inputs.

glossary.definition: In a single layer perceptron (Hopgood, 2001; Jones, 2009; Russell & Norvig, 2010): there is an input layer that does not contain computing neurons and thus does not make processing; there is an output layer containing the computing neurons and providing the final output of the network. The title of the network – “a single layer perceptron” – emphasizes that the network has only one processing layer; all the inputs are connected directly to the outputs.

Therefore, a feed-forward neural network has greater processing power, and it can model practically any function of arbitrary complexity, where the number of inputs and the number of hidden layers determine the function complexity (Jones, 2009).

glossary.definition: Artificial neural networks have many hyperparameters: the number of layers, the number of processing units, the function for combining input signals, and the activation function to be used.
glossary.sentence: Artificial neural networks have many hyperparameters: the number of layers, the number of processing units, the function for combining input signals, and the activation function to be used.

glossary.sentence: Each processing unit calculates the weighted sum, including the bias input, and then applies the activation function.

glossary.definition: the knowledge base includes IF... rules (Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011).
glossary.sentence: Figure 1 demonstrates five core components of a rule-based expert system (Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011): knowledge base, database or working memory, inference engine, explanation facilities, and user interface. However, additional components, such as external applications and developer interface, can be added as well. The knowledge base includes IF... rules (Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: A storage system for unrelated facts, such as purchase transactions or student grades; it does not store relational information between these facts.
glossary.sentence: The knowledge base differs from a database. The database stores unrelated facts, for example, purchase transactions or grades of students.

glossary.definition: the inference engine performs the control cycle 'Recognize-act' (Luger, 2009) to link the rules given in the knowledge base with the facts provided in the working memory (Negnevitsky, 2011).
glossary.sentence: The inference engine performs the control cycle 'Recognize-act' (Luger, 2009) to link the rules given in the knowledge base with the facts provided in the working memory (Negnevitsky, 2011). Thus, the inference engine carries out the reasoning by interpreting and applying the rules whereby the rule-based expert system reaches a solution (Hopgood, 2001; Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: A set of formal conventions and rules used to describe knowledge; they are means that allow creating a knowledge representation or description.
glossary.sentence: Knowledge representation schemas are means that offer a set of formal conventions and rules used to describe knowledge. Using them, a knowledge representation or description is created.

glossary.definition: A specialist who knows well methods and tools of artificial intelligence and has knowledge how to describe and represent knowledge in the knowledge base, but is not necessarily an expert in all problem domains.
glossary.sentence: The knowledge engineer is a specialist who knows well methods and tools of artificial intelligence, as well as who has knowledge how to describe and represent knowledge in the knowledge base. However, the knowledge engineer is not a specialist in all problem domains.

glossary.definition: An individual with deep knowledge (of both facts and rules) and substantial practical experience in a particular domain, who may provide domain-specific heuristic knowledge to solve problems more effectively.
glossary.sentence: Anyone can be considered a domain expert if he/she has deep knowledge (of both facts and rules) and substantial practical experience in a particular (usually limited) domain (Negnevitsky, 2011). Moreover, the domain expert has domain-specific heuristic knowledge that allows him to solve problems in a more effective way.

glossary.definition: An automated system used by either the domain expert or the knowledge engineer to describe knowledge and build a knowledge base; it includes tools for analysis, generation of knowledge, and checking consistency of created representations.
glossary.sentence: The knowledge acquisition system is an automated system that could be used either by the domain expert directly or by the knowledge engineer to describe knowledge and to build a knowledge base. Such systems can offer a variety of user interfaces, for example, they could offer creation of diagrams representing some scenarios from the problem domain or the creation of structured description of knowledge. Moreover, such systems include a set of tools used for the analysis and generation of knowledge and checking the consistency of a created representation.

glossary.definition: A knowledge-based intelligent system that solves problems in a narrowly specialised domain at a performance level similar to human experts, emulating their methodology and performance.
glossary.sentence: Intelligent computer systems that emulate the human expert’s methodology and performance are called expert systems (Luger, 2009). An expert system is a knowledge-based intelligent system that solves problems in a narrowly specialised domain in a way and at a performance level similar to one of the human experts (Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky, 2011).

glossary.definition: A type of knowledge representation schema that represents knowledge in the form of logical statements using constants, variables, functions, predicates, logical operators, and applies inference rules to derive new true knowledge from a set of true knowledge.
glossary.sentence: Several categories of knowledge representation schemas exist in the field of artificial intelligence (Grimm et al., 2007; Mylopoulos and Levesque, 1983): logical knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of logical statements, using constants, variables, functions, predicates, logical operators, and apply inference rules that allow getting new true knowledge from a set of true knowledge;

glossary.definition: A type of knowledge representation schema that represents knowledge in the form of a directed labelled graph where each node displays a simple object/concept and directed labelled arcs represent relations between them.
glossary.sentence: Several categories of knowledge representation schemas exist in the field of artificial intelligence (Grimm et al., 2007; Mylopoulos and Levesque, 1983): ... networked knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of a directed labelled graph, where each node displays a simple object/concept and directed labelled arcs represent relations between them;

glossary.definition: A type of knowledge representation schema that uses a graph to represent knowledge but with each node displayed as a complex structure containing all characteristics and components of the object/concept.
glossary.sentence: Several categories of knowledge representation schemas exist in the field of artificial intelligence (Grimm et al., 2007; Mylopoulos and Levesque, 1983): ... structured knowledge representation schemas also use a graph to represent knowledge, but each node is displayed as a complex structure with all characteristics and components of the object/ concept;

glossary.term: procedural or rule-based knowledge representation schema
glossary.definition: A type of knowledge representation schema that represents knowledge in the form of a set of procedures/instructions.
glossary.sentence: Several categories of knowledge representation schemas exist in the field of artificial intelligence (Grimm et al., 2007; Mylopoulos and Levesque, 1983): ... procedural or rule-based knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of a set of procedures/instructions.

glossary.definition: Psychological theories that postulate that knowledge on different concepts is related by associations in the human mind, and these associations define the meaning of a concept in relation to other concepts.
glossary.sentence: Networked knowledge representation schemas are based on associationist theories of human mind that were developed in psychology. These theories postulate that knowledge on different concepts is related by associations in the human mind and these associations define the meaning of a concept in relation to meaning of other concepts (Luger, 2009).

glossary.definition: An experiment conducted in 1969 by Collins and Quillian to understand how people store knowledge; it involved asking people about birds and their features, recording reaction times, which showed that knowledge is stored hierarchically.
glossary.sentence: In 1969, Collins and Quillian made an experiment with aim of understanding how people store knowledge. They asked people about birds and their features and recorded reaction time. The mean reaction time changed in relation to the complexity of a sentence. This proved that knowledge is stored at different hierarchical levels and time is needed to go along the path from the activated concept to its features.

glossary.definition: knowledge stored at the level of a concept it belongs to.
glossary.sentence: The researchers also have revealed that exceptional knowledge is stored at the level of a concept it belongs to. As a result, the mean reaction time to the question “Can an ostrich fly?” was smaller than to the question “Can an ostrich breathe?” Based on the made experiment, Collins and Quillian created a semantic network that is displayed in Figure 4.

glossary.definition: a knowledge representation schema that represents knowledge as a graph: labelled nodes in this graph correspond to domain objects/concepts but directed labelled arcs – to relations between them
glossary.sentence: A semantic network is a knowledge representation schema that represents knowledge as a graph: labelled nodes in this graph correspond to domain objects/concepts but directed labelled arcs – to relations between them (Callan, 2003; Luger, 2009; Mallach, 1994; Winston, 1992).

glossary.definition: a relation “subclass-class” (also “subset-set”, “subtype-type”, “ subkind -kind”) is represented by a title of “is a” (also “is a subclass of”, “is a subset of”, “is a type of” or “is a kind of”). It allows showing hierarchical relations by relating narrower concepts to broader concepts
glossary.sentence: Several kinds of relations are used in semantic networks: A relation “subclass-class” (also “subset-set”, “subtype-type”, “ subkind -kind”) is represented by a title of “is a” (also “is a subclass of”, “is a subset of”, “is a type of” or “is a kind of”). It allows showing hierarchical relations by relating narrower concepts to broader concepts (Fig.6).

glossary.definition: a class/set/type/kind includes a set of concepts/objects that share common properties.
glossary.sentence: A class/set/type/kind includes a set of concepts/objects that share common properties.

glossary.definition: a relation “instance-class/set/type/kind” is represented by the title of “is an instance/example of”. It also allows creating hierarchical relations by relating one specific object in the problem domain to a class/set/type/kind it belongs to
glossary.sentence: A relation “instance-class/set/type/kind” is represented by the title of “is an instance/example of”. It also allows creating hierarchical relations by relating one specific object in the problem domain to a class/set/type/kind it belongs to (Fig.7).

glossary.definition: a relation “part-whole” is represented by the title of “Part of”. It allows relating parts of the object to the whole object. Without the defined parts, the object will lose some important properties or its original appearance
glossary.sentence: A relation “part-whole” is represented by the title of “Part of” (Fig.8). It allows relating parts of the object to the whole object. Without the defined parts, the object will lose some important properties or its original appearance.

glossary.definition: a relation “object-property” is represented by the title of “property/has a property of”. It allows relating objects/concepts to their properties
glossary.sentence: A relation “object-property” is represented by the title of “property/has a property of”. It allows relating objects/concepts to their properties (Fig.9).

glossary.definition: a characteristic of an object/concept that can be measured by quantitative (numerical) or qualitative (descriptive) values.
glossary.sentence: A property is a characteristic of an object/concept that can be measured by quantitative (numerical) or qualitative (descriptive) values.

glossary.definition: a relation “property-value” is represented by the title of “value”. It allows relating properties of objects/concepts to their values. This relation can come only after the relation “property/has a property of”
glossary.sentence: A relation “property-value” is represented by the title of “value”. It allows relating properties of objects/concepts to their values (Fig.10). This relation can come only after the relation “property/has a property of”.

glossary.definition: Linguistic relations (Fig.11). They allow specifying any other kind of relations (for example, like, own, travel, etc.) that cannot be represented by the previously mentioned types of relations
glossary.sentence: Linguistic relations (Fig.11). They allow specifying any other kind of relations (for example, like, own, travel, etc.) that cannot be represented by the previously mentioned types of relations.

glossary.definition: Inheritance is a process by which a narrower node automatically inherits knowledge from the network, that is, a subclass/subset/subtype/ subkind node inherits knowledge from a class/set/type/kind node or an instance inherits knowledge from a subclass/subset/subtype/ subkind node or a class/set/type/kind node
glossary.sentence: Inheritance is a feature of semantic networks (Luger, 2009). Inheritance is a process by which a narrower node automatically inherits knowledge from the network, that is, a subclass/subset/subtype/ subkind node inherits knowledge from a class/set/type/kind node or an instance inherits knowledge from a subclass/subset/subtype/ subkind node or a class/set/type/kind node.

glossary.definition: Structured knowledge representation schemas also use a graph to represent knowledge. However, comparing to the networked knowledge representation schemas, each node in the graph represents not a single concept/object, but a complex structure describing an object/concept together with its properties and parts.
glossary.sentence: Structured knowledge representation schemas also use a graph to represent knowledge. However, comparing to the networked knowledge representation schemas, each node in the graph represents not a single concept/object, but a complex structure describing an object/concept together with its properties and parts.

glossary.definition: Frames are an example of structured knowledge representation schemas.
glossary.sentence: Frames are an example of structured knowledge representation schemas.

glossary.definition: Artificial Intelligence. Palgrave Macmillan.
glossary.sentence: Callan R. (2003). Artificial Intelligence. Palgrave Macmillan.

glossary.definition: A demon is a procedure that is invoked as a side effect of some other action in the knowledge base (Luger, 2009). Demons are triggered automatically when a slot value is accessed (Mallach, 1994). Thus, demons control actions for slot-level manipulations (Jones, 2009). Demons are usually defined in class and sub-class frames and then inherited and executed in instance frames. Object-oriented frames are acquired when demons are assigned to frames. There are standard demons defined for frames, although different shells for creating frame-based knowledge bases can offer a slightly different set of demons with different titles. The common demons are: IF-NEEDED – it is triggered when the slot value is unspecified, and it is necessary to calculate/to set it; IF-CHANGED – it is triggered when a slot value changes; IF-ADDED – it is triggered when a slot value is unspecified, and it is added to the frame; IF-REMOVED – it is triggered when a slot value is deleted. Although the demons are triggered automatically, their executable part (the code describing what will happen when the demon is called) should be developed by the developer of the intelligent computer system.
glossary.sentence: A demon is a procedure that is invoked as a side effect of some other action in the knowledge base (Luger, 2009). Demons are triggered automatically when a slot value is accessed (Mallach, 1994). Thus, demons control actions for slot-level manipulations (Jones, 2009). Demons are usually defined in class and sub-class frames and then inherited and executed in instance frames. Object-oriented frames are acquired when demons are assigned to frames. There are standard demons defined for frames, although different shells for creating frame-based knowledge bases can offer a slightly different set of demons with different titles. The common demons are: IF-NEEDED – it is triggered when the slot value is unspecified, and it is necessary to calculate/to set it; IF-CHANGED – it is triggered when a slot value changes; IF-ADDED – it is triggered when a slot value is unspecified, and it is added to the frame; IF-REMOVED – it is triggered when a slot value is deleted. Although the demons are triggered automatically, their executable part (the code describing what will happen when the demon is called) should be developed by the developer of the intelligent computer system.

glossary.definition: the control cycle 'Recognize-act' makes the basis of the inference mechanism.
glossary.sentence: The control cycle “Recognize-act” makes the basis of the inference mechanism. It implements a pattern-directed search in the knowledge base by searching for a match between the content of the working memory and production rules.

glossary.definition: explanation facilities are essential for any expert system because an expert system provides the user with a recommendation, plan, advice, conclusion, or any other result.
glossary.sentence: Explanation facilities are essential for any expert system because an expert system provides the user with a recommendation, plan, advice, conclusion, or any other result. However, to accept the system’s recommendation, the human expert should be aware of how a particular conclusion was reached, what was the line of reasoning and whether it is valid or not (Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011). Thus, an expert system should explain its reasoning and justify the output ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: the working memory contains a description of the current state of the problem-solving process (Callan, 2003; Luger, 2009) that is called a pattern .
glossary.sentence: The working memory contains a description of the current state of the problem-solving process (Callan, 2003; Luger, 2009) that is called a pattern . The case-specific data are separated from the knowledge base (Luger, 2009). These data are assumed to be correct when they are used (Hopgood, 2001).

glossary.definition: rules used by human experts that must be preserved in building a knowledge base; they dictate a specific sequence of actions or checks for problem-solving, such as 'if the engine does not turn over and the lights don’t come on, then check the battery'.
glossary.sentence: the structure of rules used by the human experts must be preserved in building a knowledge base. For example, when a mechanic says, “if the engine does not turn over and the lights don’t come on, then check the battery” , he/she suggests a specific sequence of things to try.

glossary.definition: a rule-based expert system must keep track of the current state of the problem-solving process (case-specific data) and the working memory ensures this by storing the facts acquired from the user, conclusions derived in the reasoning process, confidence measures of conclusions, information coming from external programs, dead ends in the search process, and other information relevant to the case under consideration (Luger, 2009).
glossary.sentence: Figure 1 demonstrates five core components of a rule-based expert system (Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011): knowledge base, database or working memory, inference engine, explanation facilities, and user interface. However, additional components, such as external applications and developer interface, can be added as well.

glossary.definition: the user interacts with the system through a user interface designed as a question-and-answer, menu-driven, natural language, or graphical interface (Luger, 2009).
glossary.sentence: Figure 1 demonstrates five core components of a rule-based expert system (Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011): knowledge base, database or working memory, inference engine, explanation facilities, and user interface. However, additional components, such as external applications and developer interface, can be added as well. The user interacts with the system through a user interface designed as a question-and-answer, menu-driven, natural language, or graphical interface (Luger, 2009).

glossary.definition: a component in rule-based expert systems where facts are stored with time tags, allowing conflict resolution strategies to prioritize rules based on the recency of data; for instance, firing the rule that uses the most recently entered data first.
glossary.sentence: fire the rule that uses the data most recently entered in the working memory. This method relies on time tags attached to each fact in the working memory; thus, in the conflict set, the expert system first fires the rule whose antecedent uses the data most recently added to the working memory (Negnevitsky, 2011).

glossary.definition: a type of inference engine chosen when a domain expert first needs to gather some information and then tries to infer from it whatever can be inferred; this approach mimics the process of starting with available data and drawing conclusions from it.
glossary.sentence: If a domain expert first needs to gather some information and then tries to infer from it whatever can be inferred, choose the forward chaining inference engine.

glossary.definition: a type of inference engine chosen when a domain expert begins with a hypothetical solution and then attempts to find facts to prove it; this approach mimics the process of starting with a hypothesis and working backward to verify it with available data.
glossary.sentence: However, if your domain expert begins with a hypothetical solution and then attempts to find facts to prove it, choose the backward chaining inference engine.

glossary.definition: the set of enabled rules from which one rule is chosen to fire during conflict resolution in production systems; this set contains all applicable rules that could potentially be executed at a given time.
glossary.sentence: Conflict resolution is the method of choosing one rule to fire from those that are able to fire, i.e., from the set of enabled rules, known as the conflict set (Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: the method of choosing one rule to fire from those that are able to fire, i.e., from the set of enabled rules, known as the conflict set (Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011).
glossary.sentence: Conflict resolution is the method of choosing one rule to fire from those that are able to fire, i.e., from the set of enabled rules, known as the conflict set (Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: the structure and sequence of production rules are significant in the context of a chosen conflict resolution strategy; guidelines for structuring these rules include avoiding OR operators, limiting combinations of AND and OR, and adding only one action to the THEN part.
glossary.sentence: Both the structure and the sequence of production rules are significant in the context of a chosen conflict resolution strategy. Thinking about the structure of production rules, it is necessary to take into account the following guidelines: try avoiding the use of OR in production rules because this operator introduces ambiguity; if it is not possible to avoid using OR, try avoiding the use of combinations of AND and OR in the same rule; try adding only one action or statement/conclusion to the THEN part of production rules.

glossary.definition: systems that utilize production rules as a natural way of knowledge representation, offering advantages such as uniform structure, separation of knowledge and control, modularity, and ease of tracing and explanation; they are capable of handling incomplete and uncertain knowledge through confidence factors but have limitations in scalability and real-time performance.
glossary.sentence: Rule-based expert systems have the following advantages: Production rules are a natural way of knowledge representation because human experts very often explain problem-solving using constructions like “if... then...” ( Negnevitsky , 2011 ); Uniform structure. Production rules offer a unified way of storing knowledge because each rule is an independent unit of knowledge; thus, the very syntax of production rules enables them to be self-documented ( Negnevitsky , 2011). Separation of knowledge and control. Knowledge is stored in the knowledge base, but its processing mechanism is the inference engine. The advantages of this separation include ease of modifying the knowledge base without requiring a change in the code for program control and, conversely, the ability to alter the code for program control without changing the set of production rules (Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 200; Negnevitsky , 2011) Modularity of production rules. Rules have no syntactic interactions. Rules may only affect the firing of other rules by changing the pattern in working memory; they may not “call” another rule directly as if it were a subroutine, nor set the value of variables in other production rules. This syntactic independence supports the incremental development of expert systems by successively adding, deleting, or modifying the knowledge (rules) of the system. (the text fragment is based on the information given in (Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011) Tracing and explanation. The modularity of rules and the iterative nature of their execution make it easier to trace the execution of production rules. At each stage of the control cycle

glossary.definition: a method used in rule-based expert systems to represent and reason with incomplete and uncertain knowledge; these factors are added to rules to handle situations where information is not fully known or certain.
glossary.sentence: Most rule-based expert systems are capable of representing and reasoning with incomplete and uncertain knowledge by adding confidence factors. However, rule-based expert systems have also disadvantages ( Negnevitsky , 2011): in the case of the considerable number of production rules, it is difficult to find out the contribution of each specific rule to the problem-solving process; in each iteration, the inference mechanism searches the whole knowledge base. It could be a very computational resource-consuming process.
